title: Home & Family
shortSummary: Talk about your family members and your home using warm, everyday Hindi.
culturalNote: Family (परिवार) is the centre of Indian life, and the joint family, where parents, married children, and grandchildren share one home, is still common. Hindi has remarkably precise kinship terms: your father's brother is chacha (चाचा) while your mother's brother is mama (मामा), and there are different words for each grandparent. Elders are addressed with respect, often with ji, and younger people are not usually called by name alone by their elders. Children are doted on by the whole household, and respect for parents and grandparents is a deeply held value. When you visit someone's home, removing your shoes at the door and accepting at least a little tea or food is the polite thing to do, as refusing outright can seem cold.

keyPhrases.english: How many children do you have?

grammar.explanation: Possessives agree with the thing owned, not the owner. Mera (my) becomes meri before feminine nouns and mere before plural or oblique nouns: mera bhai (my brother), meri behan (my sister), mere bhai (my brothers).
grammar.commonMistake: Using mera with a feminine noun; it should change to meri, as in meri behan.

grammar.explanation: Out of respect, parents and elders take plural verb forms even when there is only one person. Mere pitaji hain (my father is) uses hain, the plural, rather than the singular hai.

examples.native: ये मेरे पिताजी हैं। (ye mere pitaji hain.)
मेरी माँ खाना बनाती हैं। (meri maa khaana banaati hain.)

grammar.commonMistake: Using hai (singular) for a parent; respect calls for the plural hain.

dialogue.setup: Sam visits his Indian friend Arjun's home and is introduced to the family.

lines.speaker: Sam
lines.native: नमस्ते! आपका घर बहुत सुंदर है।
lines.transliteration: namaste! aapka ghar bahut sundar hai.
lines.english: Hello! Your house is very beautiful.

lines.speaker: Arjun
lines.native: ये मेरे पिताजी और माँ हैं।
lines.transliteration: ye mere pitaji aur maa hain.
lines.english: These are my father and mother.

lines.speaker: Sam
lines.english: Hello! How many children do you have?

lines.speaker: Arjun
lines.native: हम तीन भाई-बहन हैं। हम एक साथ रहते हैं।
lines.transliteration: hum teen bhai-behan hain. hum ek saath rehte hain.
lines.english: We are three siblings. We live together.

lines.speaker: Sam
lines.native: बहुत अच्छा! आपका परिवार बड़ा है।
lines.transliteration: bahut achha! aapka parivaar bada hai.
lines.english: Very nice! Your family is big.

lines.speaker: Arjun
lines.native: जी हाँ, मेरे दादा-दादी भी यहाँ रहते हैं।
lines.transliteration: ji haan, mere daada-daadi bhi yahaan rehte hain.
lines.english: Yes, my grandparents also live here.

quiz.question: How do you say 'This is my family' in Hindi?
quiz.correctAnswer: यह मेरा परिवार है (yah mera parivaar hai)
quiz.explanation: Mera agrees with the masculine noun parivaar.

quiz.question: Which possessive correctly fills 'I have ___ sister': मेरी ___ बहन?
quiz.explanation: Behan (sister) is feminine, so the possessive is meri.

quiz.question: ये मेरे पिताजी _____। (This is my father, respectful)
quiz.explanation: Elders take the respectful plural hain, not hai.

quiz.question: What does 'हम एक साथ रहते हैं' mean?
quiz.explanation: Ek saath means 'together' and rehte hain is 'live'.

quiz.question: Arrange: है / घर / बड़ा / मेरा (My house is big)
quiz.correctAnswer: मेरा घर बड़ा है (mera ghar bada hai)
quiz.explanation: Possessive, noun, adjective, then the verb hai at the end.
